name: Minho Coast
tagline: Where the Atlantic meets ancient forests and vineyards.
description: Where the Atlantic meets ancient forests and vineyards.
heroSubtitle: Portugal's greenest region, where Atlantic surf meets Vinho Verde valleys.
whyDescription:
Where Portugal begins and where most visitors never reach. Ancient forests meeting wild Atlantic beaches. Vinho Verde vineyards covering every hillside. Granite villages with Romanesque churches and family tascas serving dishes unchanged for generations.

The Minho is Portugal's greenest, most authentic region. The seafood is an hour old. The wine was made by someone you can visit. The beaches are empty on a Tuesday in July. This is not undiscovered. It is simply un touristed. And that is exactly the point.
highlights: Wild Atlantic coastline with deserted beaches
Vinho Verde wine country — the world's only green wine region
Peneda-Gerês National Park — pristine wilderness
Medieval towns and Romanesque architecture
Some of the best seafood in Portugal
seoTitle: Minho — Private Homes in Northern Portugal
seoDescription: Discover the Minho: ancient forests, wild beaches, Vinho Verde vineyards. Private homes operated to hotel standards.
howToGetHere: Fly into Porto Airport (OPO), 45 minutes north by car. Vigo Airport (VGO) in Spain is 1 hour south. We arrange airport transfers for all guests.
bestTimeToVisit: June to September for beach and outdoor adventures. May and October for hiking, wine harvest, and fewer crowds. Mild and green year round.
whatToExpect: Lush green landscapes, wild Atlantic beaches, traditional granite villages, and some of the best seafood in Portugal. A pace of life the south lost decades ago.

insiderRecommendations.name: Tasca do Celso
insiderRecommendations.description: Ponte de Lima. No menu. The owner brings what is fresh. Book the terrace.

insiderRecommendations.name: Praia do Afife
insiderRecommendations.description: Between Viana and Caminha. Wild, uncrowded, with a beach bar that grills sardines to order.

insiderRecommendations.name: Quinta de Soalheiro
insiderRecommendations.description: The best Alvarinho producer in Monção. Private tastings with the family who started it all.

insiderRecommendations.name: Trilho dos Moinhos
insiderRecommendations.description: Watermill trail in Peneda Gerês. 8km through ancient oak forest with natural swimming pools.

name: Viana do Castelo
tagline: Northern Portugal's Atlantic capital, where the Lima meets the ocean.
description: A complete guide to Viana do Castelo, the third most welcoming city in the world.
heroSubtitle: Northern Portugal's Atlantic capital, where the Lima meets the ocean

pullQuote.text: The beaches of Viana do Castelo are some of the most beautiful in the country.

primaryAccolade.text: Third most welcoming city in the world
primaryAccolade.note: First Portuguese city ranked in the global top three

whyDescription:
Few visitors travel this far north. Most stop at Porto, ninety minutes south, or detour for the Algarve. Those who continue along the coast find a working Atlantic city built on cod fishing and Eiffel-trained engineering, with seventeenth-century townhouses leaning over granite squares and, in late August, one of the largest traditional festivals in Portugal.

Viana do Castelo sits where the Rio Lima reaches the Atlantic, about seventy-five kilometres north of Porto. The municipality counts roughly eighty-six thousand residents and runs from the open ocean to the wooded edges of the Peneda mountains. It received its royal charter from King Afonso III on 18 June 1258 and became a city in 1848. For most of its history it looked outward, to the sea: shipyards on the river, codfishing fleets running the long voyage to the banks of Newfoundland, sugar trade with the Brazilian northeast, and an ironwork tradition that produced the gold-filigree jewellery still worn at the city's main festival.

The civic heart is the Praça da República, a Renaissance square that has held the same function for more than five hundred years. The fountain at its centre dates to 1554. The fortress-like former town hall and the loggia of the Casa das Varandas, built in 1589 for the Misericórdia brotherhood, stand on opposite sides. The granite is dark, the proportions human, the scale closer to a Tuscan hill town than to anything else in Portugal.

Above the city, on the Monte de Santa Luzia, a neo-Byzantine sanctuary commands the Lima estuary from two hundred and fifty metres. Designed by the architect Miguel Ventura Terra and built between 1904 and 1943, the basilica is reached by Portugal's longest funicular — six hundred and fifty metres of track, inaugurated in 1923 — or by a path of one hundred and sixty-eight steps. The view from its esplanade, of the river meeting the ocean and the town laid out below, was described by National Geographic Magazine in 1927 as rivalled in the world only by those of Rio de Janeiro and Funchal.

The waterfront has been worked, not curated. The Gil Eannes, a 1955 hospital ship built in the local shipyards to support the cod fleet on the Newfoundland and Greenland banks, is moored on the riverfront as a museum. The Ponte Eiffel, drawn in Gustave Eiffel's Paris office and inaugurated on 30 June 1878, was the first two-level iron bridge over the Lima and remains in daily use, carrying both road and rail. The Centro Cultural, designed by the Pritzker laureate Eduardo Souto de Moura, sits a short walk upriver, part of a small ensemble that also includes work by Fernando Távora and Álvaro Siza.

The setting works in seasons. The Atlantic stays cool, between sixteen and eighteen degrees in summer, which makes Praia do Cabedelo — a one-kilometre crescent of fine sand reached by a five-minute river ferry — one of the leading windsurf and kitesurf beaches in Portugal. Praia Norte, in the city itself, holds Blue Flag status and two saltwater tidal pools, one shallow enough for children. Inland, the Lima valley runs east through one of the original sub-regions of the Vinho Verde appellation, the birthplace of the Loureiro grape, with manor estates around Ponte de Lima opening for tastings.

The town is at its most itself in the third week of August, during the Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia. The festival has been held since 1772. Boats are decorated and taken in procession to the sea. Women appear in the gold-filigree costume of the Alto Minho, which the local jewellers' tradition still produces. Fireworks are launched over the Lima. The accommodation books out months in advance.

What the rest of the year offers is quieter. Tap-water espresso at granite cafés. Friday market under the bridge. A funicular climb at dusk. Cod fritters at a marble counter. A drive twenty minutes inland to find a quinta that has been making white wine from Loureiro vines for four generations.

Most travellers who come to Northern Portugal stop short of Viana. The ones who continue find that the city has stayed on its own scale, more Atlantic than Mediterranean, and that its accolade — third most welcoming city in the world, ranked by Booking.com in 2024 from more than three hundred million verified reviews — was earned by the same restraint that keeps it interesting.
highlights: Third most welcoming city in the world (Booking.com Traveller Review Awards 2024)
Basílica de Santa Luzia — Portugal's longest funicular climbs 650m to the cupola
Praia do Cabedelo — one of Portugal's leading windsurf and kitesurf beaches
Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia, held annually since 1772
Gateway to the Vinho Verde appellation and the Loureiro grape's place of origin
seoTitle: Viana do Castelo: Northern Portugal's Atlantic Capital | Portugal Active
seoDescription: A complete guide to Viana do Castelo, the third most welcoming city in the world. Atlantic beaches, Vinho Verde valleys, and our curated villas in the Minho.
howToGetHere: Porto airport (OPO) 45–50 min by A28 (toll-free through Viana since Jan 2025). CP intercity from Porto Campanhã 1h15–1h25. Vigo airport 1h from the Spanish side. Lisbon is 4h by motorway.
bestTimeToVisit: Late May to June and September. Warm, drier weather (20–25°C), vineyards either at their greenest or at full vindima, and crowds that are manageable rather than absent.
whatToExpect: A working Atlantic city, more Atlantic than Mediterranean — fishing boats at dawn, basilica at sunset, Vinho Verde at every table.
whereToStayIntro: Portugal Active manages a curated portfolio of homes in and around Viana do Castelo, from restored merchant townhouses in the historic centre to private quintas in the Lima valley and Atlantic-facing properties on the coast. Each home is operated to hotel standards: a chef is on call, the concierge holds tables that walk-in guests cannot, and the property is verified against a one-hundred-and-forty-seven-point checklist before every arrival. The selection below is a starting point. The full collection is searchable by capacity, dates, and proximity to the historic centre, the river, or the ocean.
journalSectionTitle: From the journal — stories from Viana do Castelo
thingsToDoIntro: What follows is what locals would recommend to a first-time visitor with three days. The historic centre is small enough to walk in an afternoon. The hilltop deserves its own morning. The waterfront is a separate world, between river and ocean. And what lies outside the city — the Lima valley, Ponte de Lima, Caminha at the Spanish border, Peneda-Gerês — is the reason most return visitors come back for a second stay.

items.name: Praça da República
items.description: The city's beating heart — a Renaissance square that has held the same function for over five hundred years. The chafariz at its centre dates to 1554; the loggia of the Casa das Varandas opposite was built in 1589 for the Misericórdia brotherhood.

items.name: Igreja da Misericórdia
items.description: A loggia of arched windows above a cloister of azulejos. Step inside for the painted ceiling alone.

items.name: Sé Catedral
items.description: Romanesque-Gothic cathedral with two crenellated towers. A working parish, not a museum.

items.name: Museu do Traje
items.description: The Minho's textile traditions in costume form — gold-filigree embroidery and ceremonial dress that still appear at the Romaria.

items.name: Santuário de Santa Luzia
items.description: Neo-Byzantine basilica designed by Miguel Ventura Terra and built between 1904 and 1943. The view from the cupola was named by National Geographic in 1927 as rivalled in the world only by those of Rio de Janeiro and Funchal.

items.name: Citânia de Santa Luzia
items.description: Iron Age Celtic-Iberian hill fort behind the basilica. Stone foundations, panoramic views, almost no other visitors.

items.name: Elevador de Santa Luzia
items.description: Portugal's longest funicular — 650m of track, inaugurated in 1923. Six and a half minutes to the top.

items.name: Navio Gil Eannes
items.description: The 1955 hospital ship built in the local shipyards to support the cod fleet on the Newfoundland and Greenland banks. Now a museum moored on the Lima riverfront.

items.name: Ponte Eiffel
items.description: Drawn in Gustave Eiffel's Paris office and inaugurated on 30 June 1878. The first two-level iron bridge over the Lima, still in daily use carrying both road and rail.

items.name: Praia Norte
items.description: City beach at the river mouth — Blue Flag-rated, with two saltwater tidal pools (one shallow enough for children).

items.name: Praia do Cabedelo
items.description: Across the river — one of Portugal's leading windsurf and kitesurf beaches. A five-minute river ferry runs through the day.

items.name: Centro Cultural
items.description: Eduardo Souto de Moura's contribution to the waterfront ensemble that also includes work by Fernando Távora and Álvaro Siza.

items.name: Vinho Verde valley
items.description: The Lima River valley behind Viana is one of the original sub-regions of the Vinho Verde appellation — the Loureiro grape's place of origin. Manor estates around Ponte de Lima open for tastings.

items.name: Caminha day trip
items.description: Twenty-one kilometres north on the N13 coast road — a smaller riverside town on the Minho estuary, with a walled medieval-Renaissance core and a fountain by the same stoneworker as Viana's.

items.name: Peneda-Gerês National Park
items.description: Portugal's only national park, an hour east by car. Granite villages, mountain trails, and the long-haired Garrano horses that have lived in these mountains since the Roman period.

seasons.spring: March through May. Highs climb from roughly eighteen to twenty-one degrees; rain eases but remains a feature of the calendar. The Atlantic is still cold, around fourteen degrees, and best left to surfers. What spring offers instead is the Minho at its greenest: vineyards in early leaf, camellias and wildflowers along the river, low crowds, and rates well below summer.
seasons.summer: June through August. Warm and dry, with August highs reaching twenty-six degrees and only twenty-nine to thirty-eight millimetres of rain across the month. The ocean stays cool at sixteen to eighteen degrees, swimmable for the hardy and ideal for surf, windsurf, and kitesurf at Cabedelo. Early August holds the city's two largest events — Neopop in the first week and the Romaria d'Agonia in the third — which together make this the tightest accommodation window of the year.
seasons.autumn: September through November. September stays summer-warm, around twenty-five degrees, before a sharp cooling and the return of significant rain by October. The Lima valley vindima, or grape harvest, runs from September into early October, and the vineyards turn from green to copper. Early autumn is one of the best windows in the year. By November, it is genuinely wet.
seasons.winter: December through February. Mild by European standards, with highs around fifteen degrees, but the wettest and darkest months: between one hundred and eighty and two hundred and thirteen millimetres of rain per month. The Atlantic is cold and the surf serious. The historic centre is lit for Christmas — thirty-six illuminated locations and a market that runs from early December to early January — and rates are at their lowest of the year.
seasons.bestForFirstTime: Late May to June and September. The combination is consistent across all three months: warm, drier weather with highs of twenty to twenty-five degrees, vineyards either at their greenest or at full harvest, and crowds that are manageable rather than absent. June is best for beach time and long days; September is best for atmosphere — the harvest, the slower pace, the warmth holding before the rain returns.

transport.byAir: Porto's Francisco Sá Carneiro airport, sixty-five to seventy kilometres south, is the default arrival point — forty-five to fifty minutes by car on the A28, which became toll-free through the Viana section in January 2025. Vigo airport in Spanish Galicia, about seventy-eight kilometres north, is a sensible alternative for travellers connecting via Madrid or already in Spain. Lisbon is four hours by motorway and only justified if you are starting your trip in the capital.
transport.byTrain: Comboios de Portugal runs direct services on the Linha do Minho from Porto Campanhã. Faster trains cover the route in one hour and fifteen to twenty-five minutes, with departures roughly hourly at peak. Fares range from eight to sixteen euros one-way. Viana station sits eight hundred metres from the historic centre — a ten-minute walk, or two minutes by taxi.
transport.byCar: From Porto, the A28 covers the seventy kilometres in forty-five to fifty minutes and is largely toll-free near Viana, with only the Angeiras and Póvoa gantries closer to Porto still active. The coastal N13 is slower and more scenic, threading through fishing villages and beach towns. Drivers in vehicles with foreign plates should register for Easytoll or Via Verde before crossing the border to avoid manual toll collection.
transport.fromSpain: The most direct route from Galicia is the A3 motorway, crossing into Portugal at Valença-Tui — one hour from Vigo, one hour and forty-five minutes from Santiago de Compostela. ALSA and FlixBus run buses on the same axis with a transfer at Valença. The Caminha-A Guarda ferry has been suspended since 2020, with a 2026 reopening discussed but not confirmed; the Xacobeo Transfer water taxi currently substitutes for pilgrims walking the Caminho Português da Costa.
transport.gettingAround: The historic centre is compact and walkable. The Ecovia do Lima cycle path follows the riverbank for seventy kilometres, the most direct way into the Vinho Verde valley by bicycle. Parking in the centre is metered; the main municipal car park is at Campo d'Agonia, with roughly one thousand and sixty spaces, near the waterfront. Uber operates but thinly compared to Porto or Lisbon. A car is unnecessary for the city itself, useful for the valley and the coast.

eatDrinkIntro: Viana does not have the Michelin density of Porto or Lisbon, but it has what those cities have largely lost: working-day restaurants run by the same families for three or four generations, fish that arrived that morning, and a generation of younger chefs returning to take regional cooking somewhere new. Two restaurants in the city hold Michelin Bib Gourmand status. The Pousada de Santa Luzia, on the hilltop, has the view. Below the bridge, on the river, the wholesalers sell to the restaurants in the morning and feed visitors in the evening.

restaurants.name: Tasquinha da Linda
restaurants.description: Owned by fish wholesalers. The freshest seafood in town. Reservation essential.

restaurants.name: Camelo
restaurants.description: Bib Gourmand-recognised. Modern Portuguese cooking that leans on Minho-grown ingredients and Vinho Verde from the valley behind the city.

restaurants.name: Os 3 Potes
restaurants.description: Sixteenth-century tavern serving regional classics — arroz de polvo, bacalhau à Viana, leitão à Bairrada.

restaurants.name: A Laranjeira
restaurants.description: Family-run, no menu. Whatever came in on the boat that morning, cooked the way the owner's grandmother taught her.

restaurants.name: Pousada de Santa Luzia
restaurants.description: Hilltop dining at the basilica, with the same panorama National Geographic put in the same league as Rio de Janeiro. Reservations essential at sunset.

restaurants.name: Zé Natário
restaurants.description: Classic granite-counter pastelaria — cod fritters, bola de Berlim, milk-fed cinnamon coffees taken standing.

specialties.name: Bacalhau à moda de Viana
specialties.description: Salted cod baked with peppers, onions, potatoes and broa — the city's signature plate, on most menus year-round.

specialties.name: Arroz de polvo
specialties.description: Slow-cooked octopus rice; the Alto Minho version is wetter than the south's.

specialties.name: Lampreia do Lima
specialties.description: River lamprey, in season from January through April. A dish that polarises — those who love it return for it.

specialties.name: Vinho Verde Loureiro
specialties.description: The Lima valley is the Loureiro grape's place of origin. Crisp, low-alcohol, citric — drunk young and cold.

experiencesIntro: What follows is a selection of experiences that the concierge organizes, alone or in combination, for guests staying in Portugal Active homes. Each can be arranged with twenty-four hours' notice, most with less. None requires a car: the concierge handles transfers, reservations, and (where relevant) the introductions to growers, winemakers, and instructors who do not generally accept walk-ins.

experiences.name: Vinho Verde tasting in the Lima valley
experiences.description: A morning at a working quinta in the Loureiro grape's place of origin — typically four to six wines tasted with the producer, paired with regional cheese and bread. Twenty to thirty minutes from the city by car.

experiences.name: Atlantic surf or windsurf at Cabedelo
experiences.description: Private or small-group lessons at one of Portugal's leading windsurf and kitesurf beaches. Wetsuits and equipment included. Suitable for beginners through advanced; conditions best from late spring through early autumn.

experiences.name: Kayaking the Lima estuary
experiences.description: From the marina upriver toward the wooded banks above the Eiffel bridge, with a stop at a small beach for swimming when conditions allow. Guided by a local instructor.

experiences.name: Day trip to Caminha and the Spanish border
experiences.description: Twenty-one kilometres north to a smaller riverside town on the Minho estuary, with a walled medieval-Renaissance core, a fountain from the same stoneworker as Viana's, and a view across to Galicia. Combined easily with a lunch stop on the way back.

experiences.name: Peneda-Gerês National Park
experiences.description: Portugal's only national park, an hour by car inland: granite villages, mountain trails, and the long-haired Garrano horses that have lived in these mountains since the Roman period. Best done as a guided private day.

experiences.name: Funicular ride to Santa Luzia at golden hour
experiences.description: The classical evening: the six-and-a-half-minute climb to the sanctuary, the panorama of the river meeting the ocean, dinner at the Pousada restaurant with the same view. Reservations are essential.

eventsIntro: Two events dominate Viana's calendar, both in August, both worth planning either toward or around. Several smaller events through the year offer reasons to visit out of season — the spring blues festival on the Praça, the autumn vindima, the Christmas illuminations in the historic centre. Accommodation in Viana follows the festival calendar more sharply than elsewhere in the Norte.

events.name: Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia
events.description: The city's flagship festival, held annually since 1772. Boat processions, gold-filigree costume parades, fireworks over the Lima. Accommodation books out months in advance.

events.name: Neopop Electronic Music Festival
events.description: International electronic music on the waterfront. The first week of August is the tightest accommodation window of the year together with the Romaria.

events.name: Wine & Blues Festival
events.description: Vinho Verde producers and blues acts on the Praça da República. The unofficial start of the spring travel season.

events.name: Vindima do Vinho Verde
events.description: Grape harvest across the Lima valley. Many quintas open their doors for working-harvest visits.

events.name: Christmas illuminations
events.description: Thirty-six illuminated locations in the historic centre, with an artisan market under the Eiffel bridge.

events.name: Carnaval
events.description: Smaller and more traditional than Torres Vedras or Madeira, but characteristically Minho — masked figures, regional brass bands, sardines on the grill.

pressIntro: The city has been written about consistently but not heavily — which is part of its appeal. The selection below is drawn from English-language travel coverage of the past decade and from the Booking.com global ranking that placed Viana, in 2024, as the third most welcoming city in the world.

pressQuotes.quote: Third most welcoming city in the world.
The beaches of Viana do Castelo are some of the most beautiful in the country.
A working Portuguese city the tourist trail forgot.
The Minho's Atlantic capital quietly turning into one of Portugal's most rewarding small cities.
Viana do Castelo is having its quiet moment — go before that changes.

faqs.question: How many days do I need in Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Two to three days suit most first-time visitors: one for the historic centre and the Santa Luzia hilltop, one for the beaches and waterfront, and a third for day trips into the Lima valley or to Ponte de Lima. As a base for the wider Alto Minho — including Peneda-Gerês and the Vinho Verde quintas — four to five days is the comfortable length.

faqs.question: What is the best time to visit Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Late May or September. The Atlantic is at its warmest, the city is full but not crowded, and the restaurant calendar runs at full strength. Mid-August coincides with the Romaria d'Agonia — book a year ahead if you want to be here for it.

faqs.question: Is Viana do Castelo worth visiting from Porto?
faqs.answer: Yes — and it is a different experience. Porto is a busy compact city; Viana is a working coastal town with empty beaches, granite squares and a slower rhythm. The train takes 75 minutes, so day-trips are easy, but two nights does the city justice.

faqs.question: What is Viana do Castelo famous for?
faqs.answer: Three things. The Basílica de Santa Luzia on the hill above the city. The Romaria de Nossa Senhora d'Agonia, one of Portugal's most important religious festivals. And the cod-fishing fleet — the Gil Eannes hospital ship moored on the Lima river is a working museum to that history.

faqs.question: How do I get from Porto to Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Comboios de Portugal runs an intercity train from Porto Campanhã to Viana in 75 minutes for €8.90 one way. By car, the A28 motorway takes one hour. The scenic alternative — the N13 coast road — adds 30 minutes.

faqs.question: Where are the best beaches in Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Praia Norte for families (calm, breezy, lifeguarded). Cabedelo across the river for surfers and walkers (five kilometres of empty sand). Praia do Afife, twenty minutes north, for the wildest stretch.

faqs.question: What language is spoken in Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Portuguese. English is widely spoken in restaurants, hotels and shops in the historic centre. Spanish is understood by most adults; French by older generations.

faqs.question: Is Viana do Castelo safe for families?
faqs.answer: Yes — among the safest small cities in Europe. Praia Norte is lifeguarded in summer. The historic centre is walkable, well-lit and pedestrianised. Healthcare is excellent (Hospital de Santa Luzia is five minutes from any of our properties).

faqs.question: What is the typical food in Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: Bacalhau à moda de Viana (salted cod, baked with peppers and onions). Arroz de polvo (octopus rice). Lampreia in season (river lamprey, January–April). Sardines on the grill all summer. Vinho Verde — always cold, always young.

faqs.question: How far is Viana do Castelo from the airport?
faqs.answer: Porto airport (OPO) is 70 kilometres south — one hour by car on the A28 motorway. We arrange private transfers from OPO to any of our Viana properties.

faqs.question: What are the best villas to rent in Viana do Castelo?
faqs.answer: We operate twelve properties in and around Viana — from restored stone farmhouses in the Lima valley to contemporary villas above the Cabedelo dunes. All include daily housekeeping, local concierge and private chef on call. See our Viana collection above.

ownersCTA.headline: Own a home in Viana do Castelo?
ownersCTA.body: Portugal Active manages premium properties across the Alto Minho: full-service management, marketing, concierge, and maintenance, operated to hotel standards. Our owners earn, on average, thirty percent more than self-managed properties in the region.

name: Caminha
tagline: Fortified border town facing Galicia across the Minho estuary.
description: [TBD: deep research output] Border town at the mouth of the Minho river, the northernmost point of Portugal's Atlantic coast.
heroSubtitle: Where Portugal meets Galicia across an estuary of empty beaches.
whyDescription: [TBD: Cowork deep-research output to fill — 250-350 word editorial. Caminha's fortified medieval centre, the Atlantic-facing Praia Moledo, day-ferry to Tui in Galicia, the Coura valley behind the town. Paredes de Coura festival reference for cultural anchor.]
highlights: Medieval walled centre and Torre do Relógio
Praia do Moledo and Camarido pine forest
Day-ferry to Tui (Spain) across the Minho estuary
Paredes de Coura music festival, 20 min inland

seoDescription: [TBD] A complete guide to Caminha — medieval walls, Moledo beach, and ferries to Galicia. Stay in our curated homes on the Minho coast.
howToGetHere: Porto airport (OPO) 1h15 by A28. Vigo (Spain) 45 min. Viana do Castelo 25 min south.

Praia de Ofir and Praia da Apúlia
Cávado river estuary — paddleboard and kayak

howToGetHere: Porto airport (OPO) 40 min by A28. Viana do Castelo 25 min north. Braga 25 min east.

name: Porto and Douro
tagline: The city, the river, the wine.
description: The city, the river, the wine.
heroSubtitle: The city that gave Portugal its name, and the river that built it.
whyDescription:
The city that gave Portugal its name. Port wine cellars lining the riverbank. Michelin starred restaurants in converted warehouses. A creative energy that makes Lisbon feel mainstream. Porto is compact, walkable, and endlessly rewarding for those who take the time to explore beyond the postcard views.

Beyond the city, the Douro Valley unfolds in terraced vineyards classified as a UNESCO World Heritage landscape. This is where port wine comes from, but it is also where Portugal's most exciting table wines are being made. Visit a quinta, taste with the winemaker, and understand why this region is considered one of Europe's most beautiful.
highlights: UNESCO World Heritage city centre
Port wine cellars and Douro Valley vineyards
Michelin-starred dining scene
Ribeira waterfront and iconic bridges
Gateway to the Douro wine region
seoTitle: Porto — Private Homes in Porto and Douro Valley
seoDescription: Discover Porto: historic city, port wine, Douro Valley. Private homes operated to hotel standards.
howToGetHere: Fly into Porto Airport (OPO), 20 minutes from city centre by metro or taxi. High speed train from Lisbon takes 2h45.
bestTimeToVisit: May to October for warm weather. September for wine harvest in the Douro. Year round for the city.
whatToExpect: Compact, walkable city with world class food, port wine culture, and creative energy. The Douro Valley is 90 minutes east by car.

insiderRecommendations.name: The Yeatman
insiderRecommendations.description: Two Michelin stars overlooking the Douro. The tasting menu with wine pairing is extraordinary.

insiderRecommendations.name: Livraria Lello
insiderRecommendations.description: Go at opening time to avoid crowds. The staircase alone is worth the visit.

insiderRecommendations.name: Quinta do Vallado
insiderRecommendations.description: One of the oldest estates in the Douro. Stay overnight and wake up to terraced vineyards.

insiderRecommendations.name: Foz do Douro
insiderRecommendations.description: Sunset walk along the ocean promenade. End at Praia do Carneiro for a drink at Tavi.

name: Douro Valley
tagline: UNESCO World Heritage vineyards on terraced hillsides above the river.
description: [TBD] The world's oldest demarcated wine region, where port and Portugal's most exciting table wines are made.
heroSubtitle: Europe's most photographed wine landscape — and the most rewarding to actually stay in.
whyDescription: [TBD: Cowork deep-research output. UNESCO World Heritage landscape, port-wine quintas, the Pinhão area, vintage and harvest seasons, river cruises, Régua railway, dining at Yeatman and DOC.]
highlights: UNESCO World Heritage cultural landscape
Centuries-old port wine quintas
Pinhão and Régua river towns
Vindima harvest season (September)
Riverboat cruises and the Linha do Douro railway
seoTitle: Douro Valley, Portugal: UNESCO Wine Country | Portugal Active
seoDescription: [TBD] A complete guide to the Douro Valley — terraced vineyards, port quintas, river cruises and our curated stays.
howToGetHere: Porto airport (OPO) 1h30 to Pinhão. Linha do Douro train from Porto São Bento, 2h15.
bestTimeToVisit: [TBD] September for vindima; April-June for green vineyards.

name: Lisbon
tagline: Golden light, coastal charm, and timeless elegance.
description: Golden light, coastal charm, and timeless elegance.
whyDescription: Europe's sunniest capital, where cobblestone hills meet the Atlantic. Lisbon is a city of contrasts — ancient trams climbing through Alfama's narrow streets while Michelin-starred chefs reinvent Portuguese cuisine in converted palaces. The coastline stretches from the surf breaks of Ericeira to the glamour of Cascais, with Sintra's fairy-tale palaces just 30 minutes away.
highlights: Europe's sunniest capital with 300 days of sun
Cascais and Sintra — coastal glamour meets fairy-tale palaces
World-class gastronomy and wine scene
Rich maritime history and cultural heritage
Surf, sailing, and beach culture year-round
seoTitle: Lisbon — Private Homes in Lisbon and Coast
seoDescription: Discover Lisbon: golden light, coastal charm, timeless elegance. Private homes coming soon.
howToGetHere: Fly into Lisbon Airport (LIS), 30 minutes from the city centre. Cascais and Sintra are 30-40 minutes west by car or train.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June and September to October for perfect weather. July-August is peak season with higher prices.
whatToExpect: Golden light, pastel-coloured buildings, world-class pastéis de nata, and a coastline that stretches from surf breaks to glamorous beach clubs.

insiderRecommendations.name: Belcanto
insiderRecommendations.description: José Avillez's two-Michelin-star flagship. Modern Portuguese cuisine at its absolute best.

insiderRecommendations.name: Praia da Ursa
insiderRecommendations.description: Hidden beach near Sintra. A steep 20-minute hike down, but you'll have dramatic rock formations almost to yourself.

insiderRecommendations.name: Feira da Ladra
insiderRecommendations.description: Tuesday and Saturday flea market in Alfama. Arrive early for the best vintage finds.

insiderRecommendations.name: Palácio da Pena
insiderRecommendations.description: Sintra's colourful hilltop palace. Go at 9am opening to beat the crowds.

name: Alentejo
tagline: Rolling plains, cork oaks, and the slowest pace in Europe.
description: Rolling plains, cork oaks, and the slowest pace in Europe.
whyDescription: Portugal's vast interior, where time moves differently. The Alentejo is a land of golden wheat fields, ancient cork oak forests, and whitewashed villages that haven't changed in centuries. The coastline — the Rota Vicentina — is one of Europe's last truly wild stretches of shore, with dramatic cliffs and empty beaches.
highlights: Rota Vicentina — Europe's wildest coastline
Cork oak forests and golden wheat plains
Dark sky reserves — some of Europe's best stargazing
Whitewashed medieval villages
Emerging wine region rivalling the Douro
seoDescription: Discover the Alentejo: rolling plains, cork oaks, wild coastline. Private homes coming soon.
howToGetHere: Fly into Lisbon (LIS), then 1.5 hours south by car. Alternatively, Beja Airport for the interior. The Rota Vicentina coast is 2-2.5 hours from Lisbon.
bestTimeToVisit: April to June for wildflowers and mild weather. September-October for wine harvest. The coast is beautiful year-round.
whatToExpect: Vast open landscapes, profound silence, dark skies full of stars, and a culinary tradition rooted in simplicity and quality ingredients.

insiderRecommendations.name: Herdade do Esporão
insiderRecommendations.description: One of the Alentejo's finest estates. Book the extended tasting with lunch at their restaurant.

insiderRecommendations.name: Praia do Malhão
insiderRecommendations.description: Wild beach on the Vicentine Coast. No facilities, no crowds — just dramatic cliffs and Atlantic waves.

insiderRecommendations.name: Monsaraz
insiderRecommendations.description: Hilltop medieval village overlooking the Alqueva reservoir. Stay for sunset — the light is extraordinary.

insiderRecommendations.name: Rota Vicentina
insiderRecommendations.description: The Fishermen's Trail section from Porto Covo to Vila Nova de Milfontes. 4 days of coastal walking perfection.

name: Algarve
tagline: Golden cliffs, private pools, and 300 days of sun.
description: Clifftop villas, private pools, and 300 days of sun.
whyDescription:
Most people know the Algarve for its resorts, golf courses, and tourist beaches. We know it for what lies beyond: the wild western coast where surfers ride empty breaks, the Monchique hills where cork oaks shade hidden trails, the fishing villages where the catch arrives twice a day and the restaurants have six tables.

The Algarve we operate in is the one that rewards curiosity. Year round sunshine, dramatic coastline, and a landscape that changes completely from east to west. Our homes and concierge team focus on the Algarve that most visitors miss entirely.

Our Algarve collection is growing. Contact our concierge for the latest availability.
highlights: 300+ days of sunshine per year
Dramatic limestone cliffs and hidden cove beaches
World-class golf courses
Fresh seafood and cataplana tradition
Vibrant nightlife in Lagos and Albufeira
seoTitle: Algarve — Private Homes in Southern Portugal
seoDescription: Discover the Algarve: clifftop villas, private pools, 300 days of sun. Private homes operated to hotel standards.
howToGetHere: Fly into Faro Airport (FAO), 30 to 90 minutes from our properties depending on location. We arrange all airport transfers.
bestTimeToVisit: Year round. Peak June to September. Spring and autumn ideal for hiking, cycling, quieter beaches. Winter mild, 15 to 18 degrees.
whatToExpect: 300+ days of sunshine, dramatic cliff coastline, world class golf, and a quieter interior that most visitors never explore.

insiderRecommendations.name: Silves Castle
insiderRecommendations.description: One of the best preserved Moorish castles in Portugal. Visit in the morning, explore the town, eat grilled fish by the river.

insiderRecommendations.name: The West Coast
insiderRecommendations.description: From Sagres to Aljezur. Wild, windswept, and the opposite of everything people expect from the Algarve.

insiderRecommendations.name: Monchique Hills
insiderRecommendations.description: Hot springs, cork forests, and hilltop villages. A completely different Algarve 30 minutes from the coast.

name: Brazil
seoDescription: Brazil is coming soon to Portugal Active.
